# Agent2

Turn domain experts into production AI agents. Agent2 clones how professionals work — their thinking, tools, reference books, memory, and judgment calls — into typed HTTP services with a stable API contract. Open the repo in an AI coding tool and run `/brain-clone` to create an expert agent through guided interview. Product code stays in agent modules; framework code stays in `shared/`.

## Design goal

Make it easy to clone domain experts into agents that:

- search and reason over professional knowledge bases
- expose a clean task API
- return structured, validated output
- pause and resume over multiple turns
- propose side effects for human approval
- keep provider affinity for cache-aware routing
- safely call scoped tools and shared knowledge services

## Public API

Every agent exposes:

- `GET /health`
- `POST /tasks?mode=sync`
- `POST /tasks?mode=async`
- `GET /tasks/{task_id}`
- `POST /tasks/{task_id}/actions/execute`

`POST /tasks` accepts `{"input": {...}}`. The input object may include:

- product-specific fields like `text`, `question`, or IDs
- `message_history` for pause/resume

Results may include:

- the typed output declared by the agent
- `_message_history` for the next turn
- `pending_actions` when host approval is required

Errors use RFC 7807 `application/problem+json`.

## Core modules

### [`shared/runtime.py`](./shared/runtime.py)

Responsibilities:

- load agent config from YAML
- resolve model IDs
- build OpenRouter-backed PydanticAI agents
- apply provider pinning through `provider_order` and `provider_policy`
- fetch optional prompts from Langfuse
- expose `create_agent()`

Important behavior:

- `instructions=` is the preferred prompt parameter
- `system_prompt=` is supported as a backward-compatible alias
- missing `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` triggers test-model mode so the API can still operate in mock mode

### [`shared/api.py`](./shared/api.py)

Responsibilities:

- expose the FastAPI app via `create_app()`
- validate request shape
- enforce auth and rate limiting
- load agent modules from Docker or source layout
- run sync and async tasks
- deserialize `message_history`
- invoke `before_run` and `after_run`
- execute pending actions through the approval workflow
- normalize failures as problem responses

The runtime recognizes these optional agent hooks:

- `before_run(input_data) -> dict`
- `after_run(input_data, output) -> None`
- `mock_result(input_data) -> dict`
- `execute_action(action) -> dict`

### [`shared/message_history.py`](./shared/message_history.py)

Framework primitive for pause/resume:

- serialize PydanticAI messages into JSON-safe data
- deserialize them back into message objects
- host applications can store this data wherever they want

### [`shared/approval_workflow.py`](./shared/approval_workflow.py)

Generic human-in-the-loop primitive:

- validates `pending_actions`
- executes one action through an injected executor
- removes completed actions from the stored result
- appends `_executed_actions`
- persists the updated run state

### [`shared/tool_policies.py`](./shared/tool_policies.py)

Tool interception layer:

- `compose_tool_policies(...)`
- `collection_scope_policy(...)`

This is where products can inject per-run scoping, validation, or argument enrichment.

## Agent configuration

`agents/<name>/config.yaml` supports:

- `name`
- `description`
- `model`
- `port`
- `timeout_seconds`
- `max_retries`
- `collections`
- `provider_order`
- `provider_policy`
- `capabilities`

The `capabilities` list is metadata for hosts and docs. It advertises which framework features an agent intends to use.

## Example agents and demos

### Product-style examples

- [`agents/example-agent`](./agents/example-agent): minimal structured-output reference
- [`agents/support-ticket`](./agents/support-ticket): ticket classification, action items, draft responses
- [`agents/code-review`](./agents/code-review): code quality analysis with structured findings
- [`agents/invoice`](./agents/invoice): invoice data extraction with tax validation
- [`agents/rag-test`](./agents/rag-test): RAG-enabled example

### Framework demos

- [`agents/approval-demo`](./agents/approval-demo): emits `pending_actions`
- [`agents/resume-demo`](./agents/resume-demo): demonstrates `_message_history`
- [`agents/provider-policy-demo`](./agents/provider-policy-demo): demonstrates provider affinity config
- [`agents/scoped-tools-demo`](./agents/scoped-tools-demo): demonstrates scoped MCP tool calls

### Template

- [`agents/_template`](./agents/_template): starting point for new agents, including hook examples

## Deployment model

### Default stack

`docker compose up -d` starts the framework core:

- Postgres
- Redis
- Langfuse
- example agents and framework demos that do not require RAG

### Full stack

`docker compose --profile full up -d` also starts:

- R2R
- Docling
- Temporal
- Temporal UI
- Knowledge MCP
- RAG and scoped-tool demos

This keeps the default developer loop fast while still supporting a full platform stack when needed.

## Knowledge and search

Knowledge is foundational to the expert cloning pattern. Domain experts do not just think -- they reference books, regulations, internal guides, and institutional memory. The Knowledge MCP server in [`shared/knowledge_mcp/`](./shared/knowledge_mcp) gives agents the same access.

Collection metadata is defined in [`knowledge/collections.yaml`](./knowledge/collections.yaml). Agents declare which collections they can search in config. Tool policies can further scope tool calls per run.

## Observability

Langfuse is optional:

- prompt management
- traces
- token and cost tracking
- eval comparisons

Prompts are code-first by default. Use Langfuse only when you want prompt registry behavior or tracing.

## Testing

Unit tests cover:

- auth and problem responses
- task API behavior
- message history serialization
- approval workflow
- provider policy wiring
- tool policy composition
- source-layout agent imports

Docker verification covers:

- compose config consistency
- default stack health
- full profile health
- end-to-end demo calls
